item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that is quite intentional. You see, OilCa is so depressed that its oil production is screwed up (this is why the Foundation actually decides to decommission it later in the article, since it doesn't serve a useful purpose and is just being a load on their resources) The Foundation is just hoping that it has potential for oil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do have a slight issue with the idea that its producing 22% of 45 liters of usable crude oil per month would help fund the Foundation in any way - it would only be worth about 5 dollars as a commodity. The mess it's making is about enough to try to make lunch at vending machine, but not much else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh. The out of order thing only applies to one entry, and that was mainly a typo that slipped through. Thanks for pointing it out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The journal entry dates being out of order make it a little confusing, unless that's what you were trying to do there? Idk, it just makes more sense to me to have them be chronological unless there's a very good reason to do otherwise.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Fixed it now:)

New Credits for Image #1: File:Lyndon B. Johnson speaking at Hemming Park- Jacksonville, Florida. Released to Wikimedia Commons with no known copyright restrictions. Retrieved from https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lyndon_B._Johnson_speaking_at_Hemming_Park-_Jacksonville,_Florida_(8079734708).jpg

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Sorry, the release on pic 1 is invalid: license laundering. That photo is indeed property of the Associated Press.

item.title: Re: +1 I liked it, but wish the ending was a bit better
item.content:encoded: Also I'd like to bring up SCP-4456-D as something that brings up the topic of decommissioned due to price costs for you. So the point really isn't as valid, because the foundation physically not being able to contain something is totally a valid reason for decommissioning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang, you really made the perfect SCP for the containment procedures you got. Impressive.

item.title: +1 for such a wild concept
item.content:encoded:
I loved moving from the containment procedures, trying to imagine what this strange thing was, to the description, which was a complete 180 from whatever erratic humanoid I was expecting.

if I could change anything, I would make the footnotes fit in a bit more organically. Reading through them feels like a breakdown of how you linked your description to the supplied containment procedures. But I also get that having it all directly in the body of the article could've made it feel convoluted.

Overall though, I love it. great concept.

item.title: Re: +1 I liked it, but wish the ending was a bit better
item.content:encoded:
You have fair points here, although this piece is set in the 70s and 80s, with an underfinanced Foundation.1 The decommissioning is a bit of commentary on owners who couldn't take care of their pets anymore.

In any case, I'm glad you enjoyed this piece!

Footnotes
1. Which is why they placed a lot of importance on SCP-5670's oil production.

item.title: +1 I liked it, but wish the ending was a bit better
item.content:encoded:
Overall I really like both the containment procedures and the SCP. I do have a bit of a problem with the ending though.

+ Open
- Close

It shifts gears from the decommissioning of the SCP to having it be really sad and broken down at the President's grave. Is it a coincidence that they choose to axe 5670 on anniversary of Johnson's death? The article ends with 5670 outside of containment in a public place. Did they finish the decommissioning? (at which point it should probably reclassified to Neutralized) Or are they going to bring it back into containment? I do have problem with the idea that attempting to neutralize the SCP because it isn't cost effective to contain it anymore. Isn't the whole point of SCP's like SCP-1609 that the foundation doesn't try to destroy things because of unintended consequences?

I get that the contest does lock your object class to keter and overall I still really like the article

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Hello everyone! This is my submission to CorpseCon, and took at least a week to complete. And what a week it has been.

This SCP is a story detailing a certain kind of relationship between a 'pet' and his master, with a focus on what happens when the 'pet' disappoints. Hope you enjoy!

Special thanks to cybersqyd, Meska, not_a_seagull, caspian2, DrAkimoto, HunterDog, plaguepjp, Penguin6, Popsioak and Ekronak for helping me polish this up!

Image Credits
Close.

1. President Lyndon B. Johnson at Cam Ranh Bay during a trip to Vietnam during the war in 1966. (AP) by manhhai. Released to Flickr under CC BY-SA 2.0.
https://www.flickr.com/photos/13476480@N07/33824525572/in/faves-188461882@N07/

2. President Lyndon B. Johnson driving an Amphicar, April 10, 1965 by Yoichi Okamoto. Released to the public domain, and can be found in Wikimedia Commons.
https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:LBJAmphicar.jpg#mw-jump-to-license

3. tunnel by Petras Gilas. Released to Flickr under CC BY-SA 2.0.
The image's upper portion has been cropped.
https://www.flickr.com/photos/gagilas/11613847064/in/faves-188461882@N07/
